1402 GMT - Air Canada's good balance of destinations and offerings has allowed it to take the edge of the worst effects of trade concerns on demand. Citi analyst Stephen Trent says investors have been rightfully focused on the risks to the carrier's cross-border flow, however, Air Canada "continues to have among the strongest weightings of lucrative, international long-haul exposure in the Americas." This is a good buffer to counter risks. "Overall, the results and guidance look positive, with Ebitda and earnings coming above consensus, solid free cash flow generation and guidance adjustments that only mildly softened the 2025 Ebitda range," Trent says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1255 GMT - Air Canada is managing what it can control after navigating turbulence of uncertainty in 1Q. Scotiabank's Konark Gupta says the Canadian flagship airline reported better-than-feared 1Q Ebitda and free cash flow thanks to its strong cost management and bookings as traffic faced headwinds. The analyst notes that management trimmed full-year Ebitda guidance, supporting Scotiabank's estimate at the low-end and consensus forecasts at the high-end. This is due to a reduced capacity target, but the company maintained its adjusted cost per available seat mile and FCF guidance thanks to cost and capex savings. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1208 GMT - Daimler Truck could post a first quarter beat on margins, but guidance could be downgraded due to weakness in heavy duty and medium duty markets, Morgan Stanley analysts write. The bank thinks earnings will come in ahead of expectations, with the industrial adjusted EBIT margin at 8.8% versus consensus at 8.5%. It sees upside driven by a strong quarter in Trucks North America. However, Daimler Truck's peers now expect a 2025 North American truck market of 275,000 units, versus 300,000 previously. The bank expects the company to adopt a similar market outlook given recent order weakness. It thinks management may guide to a 7%-9% industrial adjusted EBIT margin, down from 8%-10%. "Consensus estimates have already reduced, meaning downside on the day may be limited." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0917 GMT - Maersk maintained its earnings guidance, but the upper-end of the range looks optimistic in the current demand backdrop, JPMorgan analysts write. Maersk delivered a solid first quarter result which came in ahead of expectations, driven by better ocean freight rates as volumes were flat. Management revised downwards its market volume forecast, noting that trade uncertainty is paralysing for a lot of customers. The bank models a quarter-on-quarter deterioration in EBIT in the coming quarters, with material losses into 2026 if Red Sea disruption ends, which could drive 13% capacity growth and a 45% decline in spot rates next year. It reiterates its underweight rating and lowers its price target to 7,900 Danish kroner from 7,850 kroner. Shares trade flat at 11,575 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0846 GMT - International Consolidated Airlines has a stronger outlook than the airline group is given credit for, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ruairi Cullinane says in a research note. The view comes as the company, also known as IAG, said that it is continuing to see good demand for air travel. It posted first-quarter results in which demand for premium seats mitigated some weakness in U.S. economy demand. "We think IAG has a stronger outlook than given credit for, given a supportive capacity backdrop on the Atlantic, pricing potential from BA product measures, and given scope for business travel recovery to step up further," the analyst says. Shares trade up 2.1% at 296.34 pence. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0845 GMT - Uncertainty over the demand for third-quarter transatlantic economy flights may temper consensus upgrades for International Consolidated Airlines as the company's result for the first quarter implies around 6% upgrades for the full year, Jefferies analysts say. "We see value in IAG shares, given further self-help opportunities, relatively small carbon headwinds and strong cash generation," they say in a research note. Jefferies adds that the airline group's margins are now higher than before the pandemic, and they can keep growing as the company's long-haul capacity recovers. Shares trade up 1.4% at 294.30 pence. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0824 GMT - IAG's first-quarter earnings exceeded expectations thanks to lower fuel prices and increased revenue, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. They add that they don't expect any major changes to consensus. The company's pre-exceptional operating profit came to 198 million euros, up from expectations of 133 million euros. Despite the beat and amid continuing uncertainty, IAG maintained its guidance, adding that strength in the premium cabin is offsetting recent softness in U.S. economy leisure. "This is the first public commentary around any incremental softness on Transatlantic," the analysts say. JPMorgan adds that IAG's second-quarter revenue being ahead of the prior-year level is a positive development. Shares trade up 1.6% at 295 pence. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

data.title: Global polymer market synopsis
data.description: AsiaHDPE: HDPE prices dropped in parts of Asia. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that it would further raise output levels significantly if fellow oil-producing member countries continue to breach their quotas and engage in overproduction. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. Ongoing currency shortages, diminished demand, and reduced import offers from foreign suppliers have consistently exerted downward pressure on prices. Although sellers have been quoting lower price points, buyers have demonstrated a lack of enthusiasm in placing bids, indicative of a general market pessimism. The scenario has been exacerbated by converters exercising caution in engaging in price negotiations, which has contributed to the existing bearish sentiment. In China, following the holiday period, the pressure on upstream inventory remained minimal, which helped sustain prices. However, market sentiment became cautious as downstream purchasers hesitated to replenish their stocks, and the fundamentals of supply and demand did not show notable improvement. Shipping schedules indicate a significant influx of imports expected in mid-May, suggesting an increase in supply. Additionally, concerns arose regarding possible shipment delays following an explosion at a port in Iran, although no disruptions to May shipments have been confirmed. In the meantime, China is contemplating the exemption of approximately USD 46 billion worth of U.S. goods, including ethane, polyethylene, and styrene polymers, from tariffs. Most suppliers from the Middle East have already exhausted their May quotas, resulting in limited new pricing activity. In Southeast Asia, Polyethylene (PE) prices remained stable in spite of local producers reducing their offers in response to sluggish demand and the influx of lower-cost imports from the Middle East. Domestic producers in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand also adjusted their prices downward to maintain competitiveness. Spot market transactions remained limited, with buyers exercising caution following last week's significant price decrease. Offers for US PE were still limited, despite anticipations of increased supply stemming from US-China trade tensions. In Vietnam, market activity was temporarily halted for Labour Day holidays, while a leading producer in Indonesia further decreased contract prices from April 28, enhancing its competitiveness against imports. In the open market of India, while local producers have announced several incentive schemes to finalize Memorandums of Understanding (MoU)s, buyers have been reluctant to accept them. In India, purchasing activity remained low across the country, as buyers opted for minimal, immediate purchases rather than restocking. Demand was weak across all categories, with only a few small transactions reported. Many participants were waiting for new offers, expecting potential price reductions in the near future. Converters restricted their purchases to necessary domestic volumes, steering clear of large transactions due to a pessimistic market outlook. The combination of abundant supply and weak demand, particularly for HDPE, kept buying activity subdued. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, market activity has been restrained as a result of currency shortages and geopolitical tensions. These issues have diminished liquidity and increased uncertainty, leading buyers to restrict their purchases to only essential items and refrain from accumulating inventory, indicating a lack of confidence in the short-term stability of the market. LDPE: This week, Low density polyethylene prices slipped in parts of the Asian region. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that it would further raise output levels significantly if fellow oil-producing member countries continue to breach their quotas and engage in overproduction. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. A currency crunch situation, muted demand sentiments, and lower import offers from overseas suppliers further led to prices trending lower. In the current bearish market scenario, in spite of sellers offering low prices, buyers remained largely unwilling to bid at the stated price points. The current situation has been further exacerbated with converters exercising caution while entering into price negotiations. In China, the pressure on upstream inventory remained minimal following the holiday period, which helped maintain stable price levels. Nevertheless, market sentiment became cautious as downstream purchasers hesitated to replenish their stocks, with the supply-demand dynamics largely unchanged. Additionally, shipping schedules indicate a significant increase in imports expected around mid-May, suggesting a possible rise in supply. Concerns have also arisen regarding potential shipment delays due to an explosion at a port in Iran, although no disruptions to May shipments have been confirmed thus far. Furthermore, China is contemplating the exemption of approximately USD 46 billion worth of U.S. goods�including ethane, polyethylene, and styrene polymers�from tariffs. Most suppliers in the Middle East have already completed their May allocations, resulting in limited new pricing activity. In Vietnam, Prices of LDPE in Southeast Asia have continued to decline due to weak downstream demand, increased competition, and ongoing regional oversupply. The rerouting of U.S. polyethylene shipments from China, along with significant price cuts from Middle Eastern suppliers, has added further pressure, especially in Vietnam. In Indonesia, reductions in domestic prices have also diminished the interest in imports. Buyers have adopted a cautious approach, avoiding large purchases in anticipation of the Labour Day holidays. Overall market sentiment remains pessimistic, with further decreases expected if demand does not improve. In India, the prices of LDPE have continued to decrease due to ongoing negative market sentiment, prompting buyers to restrict their purchases to essential needs and selective restocking in order to reduce their risk of further price declines. Domestic producers are offering additional discounts to help reduce their inventory levels, yet they are struggling to sell significant quantities. Numerous processors have reduced their MOU commitments relative to the previous year, choosing instead to take advantage of the oversupplied spot market. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, LDPE market activity remained subdued, with participants limiting purchases to immediate needs amid persistent geopolitical tensions. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices down adjusted in parts of South Asia, while remaining flat in other parts of Asia. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that it would further raise output levels significantly if fellow oil-producing member countries continue to breach their quotas and engage in overproduction. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. A currency crunch situation, muted demand sentiments, and lower import offers from overseas suppliers further led to prices trending lower. In the current bearish market scenario, in spite of sellers offering low prices, buyers remained largely unwilling to bid at the stated price points. The current situation has been further exacerbated with converters exercising caution while entering into price negotiations. In India, prices remained stable as nationwide purchasing momentum had decelerated, with consumers choosing to make minimal, immediate purchases instead of restocking. Demand was weak across all categories, with only a few minor transactions reported. Many market participants were waiting for new offers, expecting possible price reductions soon. Converters restricted their purchases to essential domestic volumes, steering clear of large transactions due to a pessimistic market outlook. The combination of ample supply and weak demand, especially for HDPE and LLDPE, kept buying activity low. However, LDPE saw some support from the liquid packaging sector. Suppliers maintained a cautiously optimistic stance, pointing to rising ethane tariffs and reduced output from China as potential factors that could lead to future recovery. In the Indian domestic market, producers have announced multiple schemes and price protections to attract buyers and encourage them to complete MOUs. In China, prices have remained stable following the holiday season due to minimal pressure on upstream inventory, which has helped maintain price levels. However, market sentiment has become cautious as downstream buyers are reluctant to restock, and there has been no significant improvement in the fundamentals of supply and demand. Shipping schedules predict a considerable increase in imports around mid-May, indicating a potential rise in supply. Concurrently, US dollar-denominated offers have decreased, suggesting a possible month-on-month decline in import volumes. The U.S. dollar market prices in China are expected to experience fluctuations in the short term, leaning towards a downward trend. The discussions surrounding polyethylene import prices in China have been largely shaped by domestic market dynamics, with many importers showing a lack of optimism regarding demand due to a decrease in the consumption of plastic end-products. Furthermore, there are concerns about possible shipment delays following an explosion at a port in Iran; however, no disruptions to shipments scheduled for May have been reported. Additionally, China is contemplating the exemption of around USD 46 billion worth of U.S. goods, including ethane, polyethylene, and styrene polymers, from tariffs. Most suppliers from the Middle East have already reached their May quotas, leading to limited new pricing activity. In Southeast Asia, LLDPE prices have remained stable as local producers have lowered their offers in response to weak demand and an influx of cheaper imports from the Middle East. Domestic producers in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand have also decreased their prices to stay competitive. Spot market transactions have been limited, with buyers being cautious following last week's significant price decline. Despite the expectation of increased supply due to US-China trade tensions, offers for US LLDPE have been limited. In Vietnam, market activity was temporarily suspended for Labour Day holidays, while a major producer in Indonesia further reduced contract prices from April 28 to enhance its competitiveness against imports. Meanwhile, trading activity in Vietnam was expected to slow down further with market participants observing the Labour Day holidays from April 30 to May 4. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, market conditions were dull, with participants engaging in need-based purchases amid ongoing geopolitical tensions. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices down adjusted in the Asian region. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that it would further raise output levels significantly if fellow oil-producing member countries continue to breach their quotas and engage in overproduction. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. A currency crunch situation, muted demand sentiments, and lower import offers from overseas suppliers further led to prices trending lower. In the current bearish market scenario, in spite of sellers offering low prices, buyers remained largely unwilling to bid at the stated price points. The current situation has been further exacerbated with converters exercising caution while entering into price negotiations. In China, the polypropylene market is currently experiencing weakness due to a seasonal decline in demand and cautious purchasing behavior from downstream buyers. While maintenance outages provide temporary relief in supply, increasing inventories and anticipated capacity expansions may exert renewed pressure. The lack of new orders and inadequate terminal digestion contribute to a bearish sentiment, with prices expected to fluctuate within a limited range. The polypropylene (PP) market has shown continued signs of weakness, with futures prices declining alongside falling crude oil prices. Nevertheless, domestic spot prices have remained relatively stable, bolstered by reduced supply pressure from production halts and suppliers achieving their monthly sales targets. Major domestic suppliers, including state-owned enterprises, have upheld stable pricing strategies, resisting further downward adjustments despite the sluggish demand. Traders, anticipating uncertainty following the holiday, have begun to pre-sell cargoes scheduled for arrival post-holiday. However, buying interest has remained low, as many converters report sufficient inventory levels and choose to delay restocking, speculating on potential price declines after the holiday. Additionally, ongoing US-China trade tensions have significantly impacted sentiment, further discouraging activity in the import market. Export interest from Chinese suppliers has also been limited, with many maintaining steady offers before the holiday, yet actual transactions have been scarce as overseas buyers adopt a cautious stance. In Southeast Asia, the polypropylene (PP) market is currently experiencing a downturn, as weak demand for end products leads converters to adopt a more cautious approach. Many players are keeping their feedstock inventories low and are hesitant to make purchases unless prices become particularly appealing. Market sentiment is further affected by ongoing economic uncertainties and ambiguity regarding the results of the 90-day suspension of U.S. tariffs. Consequently, some converters are prioritizing cash flow preservation over investing in resin stockpiles. Despite the sluggish demand, supply remains plentiful�especially in domestic markets�providing converters with easy access to raw materials when necessary. This readily available supply has limited buying activity. Additionally, non-dutiable cargoes from ASEAN countries are experiencing a downward price trend, diverting attention from dutiable imports, which have seen little discussion. Dutiable suppliers are refraining from significant price reductions due to concerns over profit margins, avoiding engagement in what some perceive as an emerging price war. Chinese-origin PP cargoes, which are less competitive in Indonesia, have garnered more interest in markets such as Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam, where they benefit from non-dutiable status. However, ASEAN-origin cargoes are still favoured due to shorter shipping times. In Vietnam, purchasing activity has decreased ahead of an extended holiday, with many converters opting to observe post-holiday price movements before making commitments. Some transactions bound for Vietnam were completed at lower prices, although the decline was not as pronounced as in Indonesia. Furthermore, block copolymer PP prices in Southeast Asia are facing downward pressure. In Indonesia, local traders have reduced their offers to clear inventory, prompting importers to adjust their prices as buyers align their expectations with domestic market levels. In the Indian open market, local producers have announced several incentive schemes to finalize Memorandums of Understanding (MoU)s, while buyers have been hesitant to accept them. In India, the sentiment regarding polypropylene (PP) prices has remained subdued due to lower offers, primarily influenced by aggressive pricing strategies from Chinese suppliers. Buyers have limited their purchases to immediate needs, steering clear of bulk transactions amid a generally pessimistic market outlook. The availability of ample domestic stock has further contributed to a decline in price levels. Competitive pricing for Chinese-origin cargoes, including certain coal-to-olefins (CTO)-based products, has persisted, with transactions being finalized under advanced payment conditions. Ongoing supply pressures from China continue to significantly impact market sentiment. Looking ahead, it is anticipated that Chinese suppliers will boost exports to India prior to the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) certification deadline on June 24. However, should the enforcement of the BIS regulation be postponed�similar to previous occurrences�this may exacerbate the existing oversupply challenges. In both Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, market activity has continued to be slow due to persistent currency shortages and escalating geopolitical tensions. These economic and political challenges have limited liquidity and heightened uncertainty, prompting buyers to take a more cautious approach. Purchasing behaviour has evolved to focus solely on meeting immediate needs while steering clear of building inventory. This prudent strategy illustrates a wider market reluctance and a lack of confidence in short-term stability. PS: This week, Polystyrene prices witnessed a sharp fall in Asia. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that it would further raise output levels significantly if fellow oil-producing member countries continue to breach their quotas and engage in overproduction. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. A currency crunch situation, muted demand sentiments, and lower import offers from overseas suppliers further led to prices trending lower. In the current bearish market scenario, in spite of sellers offering low prices, buyers remained largely unwilling to bid at the stated price points. The current situation has been further exacerbated with converters exercising caution while entering into price negotiations. The Asian polystyrene (PS) market experienced a decline due to insufficient support from both cost and demand factors. Ongoing global trade tensions, which have resulted in consumption and macroeconomic uncertainties, continued to be the primary reason for the reduced buying interest in PS within the region. Demand for high-impact polystyrene (HIPS) has been more adversely affected compared to general-purpose polystyrene (GPPS) thus far. Additionally, losses in the upstream styrene monomer (SM) market have further exerted downward pressure on the PS market. The lacklustre demand has prevented the PS-SM spread from maintaining elevated levels. Some regional PS manufacturers have remained receptive to price negotiations after lowering their spot quotes in hopes of securing potential transactions. Liquidity in the spot PS market in China has remained limited. The domestic PS-SM spread in China continues to be very narrow. The plentiful and competitively priced domestic supply has led to a significant disengagement from import activities among Chinese PS users, particularly those focused on low-end downstream applications. Competitive offers from outside China are still present throughout Asia as Chinese PS manufacturers strive to sell their spot cargoes. Recently, the price of the raw material styrene has experienced fluctuations and a decline, resulting in inadequate cost support. Although production cuts have led to a reduction in supply, downstream demand has also weakened, leaving the overall supply and demand situation still loose. In the near term, the PS market is likely to be characterized by low-level sales. The polystyrene market in China has experienced a decline, influenced by reduced costs and price reductions aimed at inventory management. On the cost front, adverse macroeconomic developments have caused a drop in pure benzene prices, which in turn has led to a decrease in styrene prices. Regarding supply and demand, the United States has raised tariffs on imports from China, resulting in diminished production enthusiasm among export-oriented enterprises, and the overall pace of destocking within the industry has been sluggish, prompting companies to implement involuntary production cuts. Nevertheless, the supply and demand dynamics within the polystyrene sector remain loose, with numerous price reductions observed in the market. Furthermore, styrene production facilities are gradually resuming operations following early maintenance, leading to an increase in domestic supply while demand trends downward, potentially resulting in weak price fluctuations. The impact of U.S. tariff policies continues to hinder the recovery of downstream export demand expectations for May. Additionally, terminal enterprises are facing heightened inventory backlogs and capital turnover pressures. Given the prevailing loose supply and demand conditions, the polystyrene market is anticipated to remain predominantly weak throughout May. The demand for polystyrene (PS) in Southeast Asia has remained subdued due to ongoing uncertainties regarding global trade policies, particularly the unresolved tariff issues between China and the United States. These uncertainties have negatively impacted sentiment in end-use sectors such as packaging and home appliances, leading downstream players to be cautious about making forward purchases given the unclear consumption outlook. Currently, buyers are reluctant to engage in spot procurement. Although PS prices are at their lowest, concerns about potential further declines driven by costs, along with a lack of confidence in downstream demand, have discouraged some regional buyers from accumulating stock. Consequently, PS purchasing has primarily been driven by necessity, with few willing to commit to long positions amid the prevailing market uncertainties and significant systemic risks. On the supply side, some regional PS sellers have reduced their offers in light of the recent drop in feedstock costs. Additionally, the strengthening of several Asian currencies against the US dollar has enabled some PS exporters to provide more competitive pricing on a dollar-denominated basis, further exerting pressure on market discussions. The short-term trajectory of the market may depend on the results of post-holiday tariff negotiations between China and the US, the two principal parties involved in this round of trade disputes, as any advancements could provide clarity on the long-term outlook for global trade and consumer behavior. Moreover, trends in the upstream styrene monomer (SM) market could serve as more direct determinants influencing regional PS pricing, as current demand is insufficient to sustain PS margins at elevated levels In India, competitively priced imports from China have influenced the market. Domestic prices have decreased due to diminished cost support and a subdued demand forecast. The disparity between domestic and import prices is expected to influence procurement strategies, with buyers favoring lower-cost Chinese materials for applications sensitive to price. Pricing pressures from competitively priced Chinese materials continue to drive prices down, reflecting purchasing activity based on necessity. Currently, Chinese offers for GPPS grades are reported at USD 1000/mt levels, while bids for the same grade have been noted below USD 980-990/mt levels. Additionally, offers for HIPS grades from China have been heard at the USD 1100, with bids ranging from USD 1050-1070/mt, although there is no official confirmation. There were limited inquiries noted in the Bangladesh market, with no new transactions reported. After a rise in buying interest last week, it seems that buyers are adequately supplied for the near term. A resurgence in activity is anticipated once feedstock prices stabilize, supported by consistent demand from buyers in the export sector. ABS: ABS prices continued to fall in the Asian region this week. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that it would further raise output levels significantly if fellow oil-producing member countries continue to breach their quotas and engage in overproduction. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. Ongoing currency shortages, diminished demand, and reduced import offers from foreign suppliers have consistently exerted downward pressure on prices. Although sellers have been quoting lower price points, buyers have demonstrated a lack of enthusiasm in placing bids, indicative of a general market pessimism. The scenario has been exacerbated by converters exercising caution in engaging in price negotiations, which has contributed to the existing bearish sentiment. Asian ABS prices have declined as a result of reduced orders, with manufacturers expressing concerns regarding tariff uncertainties. Market participants have observed a drop in demand, especially from China, where U.S. orders have reportedly been cancelled due to elevated tariffs. Conversely, Southeast Asia seems to be less impacted owing to a temporary suspension of tariffs. Additionally, Chinese manufacturers are closely monitoring the forthcoming shopping festival to evaluate demand trends, which are expected to affect their production levels for June and July. On the production front, a significant ABS facility in China is functioning at diminished capacity due to weak demand, and several plants are slated for maintenance in May, which will temporarily remove a considerable amount of capacity from the market. PVC: This week, PVC prices were left unchanged across the Asian region. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that it would further raise output levels significantly if fellow oil-producing member countries continue to breach their quotas and engage in overproduction. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. This week, PVC prices in China remained stable due to weak demand and decreased trading activity, which was exacerbated by early market closures for the Labour Day holidays. Domestic spot trading was subdued as a result of declining demand and elevated inventory levels, while PVC futures experienced a downward trend following a short-lived recovery. In contrast, Indian buyers showed robust interest in the export market, aiming to acquire Chinese shipments before the implementation of new quality control regulations. PVC prices in Southeast Asia are currently stable, as the majority of market participants, including converters and Chinese sellers, had temporarily halted their activities due to Labour Day holidays. Demand in the market has been low, with few new offers available, leading many buyers to postpone their purchases. Although there is some interest from Vietnamese converters in Chinese shipments, the overall supply from suppliers such as South Korea and Japan remains limited, as they focus on other markets and manage maintenance schedules. PVC prices in India have stabilized as purchasing interest remains low, influenced by ongoing anti-dumping investigations and forthcoming quality control regulations. The majority of imports continue to originate from Chinese suppliers, utilizing either ethylene or carbide processes, with the latter experiencing decreased demand amid regulatory uncertainties. Although there is a fundamental demand�particularly as converters gear up for the monsoon season�market activity is restrained due to oversupply, impending policy changes, and a cautious stance from buyers. In the Indian open market, local manufacturers have introduced various incentive programs to finalize Memorandums of Understanding (MoUs), yet buyers have shown reluctance to accept them. The markets in Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka continue to be subdued, primarily due to currency-related limitations and a weak purchasing sentiment. Buyers in these regions are focusing solely on essential purchases and are refraining from increasing their inventory levels in light of the prevailing economic uncertainties. PET: This week, PET prices marched higher in parts of Asia while quoting steady in other parts of the region. Crude oil prices have experienced a substantial decrease, reaching their lowest level since February 2021. This drop is linked to the OPEC+ alliance approving a hike in production for June, even as Saudi Arabia issued warnings that persistent breach of quotas and overproduction from fellow oil-producing nations could trigger significant output hikes. Additionally, the market is encountering downward pressure due to bleak global demand forecasts and increasing oil inventories. PET prices have begun to firm as purchasing sentiment has improved, driven by expectations of forthcoming supply reductions anticipated at the conclusion of the second quarter. Leading manufacturers have revealed intentions to limit production to address the persistent supply surplus and uphold robust profit margins. Market players expect that the peak demand period in the northern hemisphere, especially following the holiday season, will lead to a rise in buying inquiries. Furthermore, producers of fibre- and film-grade PET have increased their prices in response to escalating feedstock expenses. Nevertheless, market insights indicate that buyers may require additional time to fully acclimate to and adjust for the recent price hikes. Some manufacturers have already started offering cargoes for June loading, while others have reported being sold out for immediate availability, as noted by market participants. In the Chinese export market, prices for PET have been increased as manufacturers attempt to offset the rising costs of inputs and production. This adjustment in pricing has resulted in diminished interest from international buyers, who are approaching the elevated prices with caution. These pricing changes indicate the cost-related pressures faced by suppliers, while the reluctance of buyers underscores the sensitivity of global demand to even slight price increases in a competitive market. The exports of bottle-grade PET from China have been bolstered by a seasonal uptick in demand, as the traditional peak season generally commences by the middle of the second quarter, enhancing purchasing activity. Meanwhile, polyester sales continue to encounter challenges due to weak domestic demand in China, and export orders are expected to be impacted by the ongoing tariff disputes. A regional PET producer is heard to have offloaded most of its shipments scheduled for delivery in May. This robust sales achievement is believed to stem from increasing anticipations of higher prices, fuelled by a reduction in spot supply within the market. The producer's approach seems to be shaped by supply-side limitations, which are expected to bolster upward price trends. Market participants indicate that this early sell-out signifies a strategic reaction to evolving supply-demand conditions and an intention to safeguard profit margins in anticipation of possible price hikes. Europe HDPE: This week, HDPE prices drifted lower in the European region. HDPE prices in Europe declined over the past week due to limited trading activity and weak demand. Market prices faced additional pressure from an increase in competitive offers. Sources within the market reported a significant lack of demand for spot volumes recently, which overshadowed the available supply. End-user demand remained low, with several consumers indicating reduced order volumes for May. The polyethylene (PE) market experienced minimal activity, largely due to a public holiday in numerous countries resulting in a shortened working week. Spot trading was subdued during the week. Seller offers contributed to the downward pressure on polyethylene prices, with competitive import offers for June further influencing the market. The ethylene monomer contract price for May was established at Euro 1,135/mt FD NEW levels, reflecting a Euro 70/mt decrease from April. This decline aligned with market expectations, attributed to a lower average naphtha price in April compared to March. Although negotiations resumed last week, market participants held varied positions regarding settlements. Some suppliers reported not offering the full decrease, while certain consumers expressed a desire to secure a complete reduction in settlements. Numerous polyethylene (PE) producers are keen to enhance their profit margins by retaining a portion of the reduction in feedstock ethylene's May monthly contract price (MCP). They cite their limited stock levels, which alleviates the urgency to sell volumes. These producers aim to capitalize on buyers' necessity to replenish their stocks during the peak demand season in spring, while also taking advantage of the fact that many buyers had previously restrained their purchasing due to the relative stability of PE contract prices, which largely resisted declines in upstream feedstock prices. This situation was primarily due to uncertainties surrounding tariffs on PE imports from the United States, which surfaced around mid-March and received approval from the majority of EU member states on April 9, only to be suspended for a period of 90 days. Consequently, a supply gap is anticipated for PE imports from the US in May and early June, resulting from the cancellation of shipments during the latter half of March and the first half of April. Additionally, with recent production challenges arising at domestic PE facilities, manufacturers are poised to exploit their constrained domestic supply. In the meantime, certain distributors have begun to reduce their prices for spot PE volumes, further compounding the reductions they implemented in April. Although it remains premature to assess the full extent of this trend, it is likely a result of expanding PE import arbitrages from the US. The uncertainty stemming from the US tariff announcements led to the cancellation of numerous PE shipments to China, thereby increasing PE supplies within the US. Trading companies managing PE imports to the EU view the 90-day tariff suspension as a chance to handle these volumes. The pricing discrepancies between these volumes and domestic spot prices appear to be significant, potentially exceeding three digits, providing trading firms with an opportunity to liquidate their existing inventories at lower prices in May while restocking in June at even more discounted rates. A large number of polyethylene (PE) facilities in Spain and Portugal experienced sudden shutdowns due to an electricity outage affecting the Iberian Peninsula. The units are gradually restarting, but the process of bringing refineries, crackers, and polymer units back to normal production levels may extend beyond a week, assuming no significant damage has occurred. Information remains limited at this time, but an initial increase in off-grade PE supply is anticipated. The closure of downstream converters' facilities, coupled with holiday breaks, has lessened any immediate supply disruptions; however, the full impact is expected to become more apparent as purchasing interest resumes in the upcoming weeks. The restoration of converters' operations is relatively straightforward compared to that of crackers and polymer units. Additionally, maintenance work has commenced at a cracker in France, while another cracker in northern France is still offline due to a separate power outage that has caused technical difficulties. With indications suggesting it may remain offline for several weeks, this situation will affect the associated PE and polypropylene (PP) units. Ongoing maintenance is also taking place at two high-density polyethylene (HDPE) plants in central and eastern Europe. In the contract markets, These developments will lead to a diverse and fragmented supply landscape in May, with the underlying inventories and production forecasts of producers being crucial factors in shaping their pricing strategies. Buyers, however, will be advocating for a complete pass-through of the reduction in feedstock ethylene's May MCP. Many are likely to seek further price reductions. Additionally, buyers will argue that the significant disparity with spot prices, including imports, necessitates a more substantial adjustment in their May contract prices. Nevertheless, producers have maintained a relatively firm stance on expanding their margins, citing the ongoing weak conditions in the petrochemical sector that pose a threat to the financial viability of PE plant operations. Beyond immediate restocking requirements, market conditions in Europe are anticipated to remain difficult, resulting in sustained low demand. This is attributed to a largely stagnant economic outlook, with the possibility of a recession still not ruled out at this point. Apart from the flexible packaging sector and a few specific applications benefiting from fast-moving consumer goods, buyers are expected to limit their purchases to essential needs and largely avoid speculative risks. While it is premature to confirm, any significant increases in upstream crude oil production rates and the resulting impact on feedstock pricing could indicate a backwardated outlook for PE pricing. Should this scenario occur, it may prompt buyers to further deplete their stocks, where feasible, with the aim of replenishing volumes at lower prices in the upcoming months. There remains some uncertainty regarding whether market conditions have stabilized at a low point and a new equilibrium is emerging, or if the slight improvement in metrics is a result of front-loading within the value chain during the 90-day pause in EU-US reciprocal tariffs. The outlook remains bleak in the UK, where businesses are implementing cost-cutting measures and manufacturing conditions are deteriorating due to weak domestic consumer confidence and low export demand. This week, spot prices for all polyethylene grades were generally lowered. Although many offers are yet to emerge, some sellers have aggressively reduced prices on medium- to large-volume lots scheduled for delivery in May, offering significant discounts compared to the spot prices observed throughout May, reflecting varying perspectives among sellers. However, these signals require further validation as the market is expected to gain traction in the upcoming weeks. LDPE: This week, LDPE prices slipped in the European region. European LDPE prices experienced a decline over the past week, attributed to a lack of trading activity and diminished demand. Market prices faced additional pressure from heightened competitive offers. Sources indicate that the insufficient demand for spot volumes during the previous week outweighed the material availability. End-user demand remained low, with some consumers reporting reduced order volumes for May. The LDPE market exhibited minimal activity, influenced by a public holiday in several countries that resulted in a shorter working week. Spot trading was subdued, and competitive import offers for June further exerted pressure on polyethylene prices. The ethylene monomer contract price for May was settled at ¬1,135/mt FD NEW levels, reflecting a ¬70/mt decrease from April. This reduction aligned with the market outlook, influenced by lower average naphtha prices in April compared to March. While negotiations resumed last week, market participants held varied positions regarding settlements. Some suppliers opted not to offer the full decrease, while certain consumers advocated for the complete decrease. A number of distributors have started to reduce their spot LDPE prices, continuing the reductions initiated in April. Although it remains premature to assess the full impact, this trend is likely influenced by the expanding LDPE import arbitrage from the US. The uncertainty stemming from recent US tariff announcements has led to the cancellation of several LDPE shipments to China, resulting in an increase in LDPE supplies within the US. Trading firms focusing on LDPE imports to the EU perceive the 90-day tariff suspension as a chance to shift these volumes. The disparity in pricing between imported volumes and domestic spot prices seems to be in the three-digit range, offering trading firms an opportunity to sell off current inventories at reduced prices in May while restocking in June at even lower rates. In addition to immediate restocking requirements, the market conditions in Europe are anticipated to remain difficult, resulting in subdued demand. The economic forecast appears to be largely stagnant, with ongoing recession risks that cannot be easily disregarded at this time. With the exception of the flexible packaging sector and a few specific applications related to fast-moving consumer goods, purchasers are likely to restrict their acquisitions to essential quantities, steering clear of speculative risks. Although it is premature to make a definitive statement, a potential rise in upstream crude oil production rates � along with its subsequent effect on feedstock pricing � could result in another backwardated pricing outlook for LDPE. Should this situation materialize, it may encourage buyers to further reduce their inventories where feasible, with the intention of restocking at lower prices in the months ahead. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices trended lower in the European region. European LLDPE prices have decreased due to low trading activity and weak demand. Market prices faced additional pressure from heightened competitive offers, as the lack of demand for spot volumes over the past week outweighed material availability. End-user demand remained sluggish, with some consumers reporting reduced order volumes for May. The LLDPE market saw limited activity, as a public holiday in several countries resulted in a shorter working week. Spot trading was subdued, and competitive import offers for June further exerted downward pressure on polyethylene prices. Many LLDPE producers are striving to enhance margins by retaining part of the reduction in the May contract price of feedstock ethylene. With limited stock availability, they experience less urgency to sell volumes. Producers are also aiming to take advantage of buyers' restocking requirements during the peak demand season in spring, especially since many buyers had delayed purchases in previous months. This postponement was mainly due to the relative stability of LLDPE contract prices despite declines in upstream feedstock costs. The tariff uncertainties regarding LLDPE imports from the US, which arose in mid-March and were approved by most EU member states on April 9, were subsequently suspended for 90 days. This situation has resulted in a supply gap for LLDPE imports from the US for May and early June, as several shipments scheduled for the latter half of March and early April were cancelled. In light of recent production challenges at domestic LLDPE facilities, manufacturers are aiming to capitalize on the constrained domestic supply to uphold their pricing strategies. This week, numerous polyethylene plants in Spain and Portugal were unexpectedly halted due to a widespread power outage across the Iberian Peninsula. Although these facilities are gradually restarting, the process of bringing refineries, crackers, and polymer units back to operational status and normalizing production could extend beyond a week, assuming no major damage is found. Information remains scarce, but an initial rise in off-grade polyethylene supply is anticipated as operations resume. The impact of the shutdown has been somewhat alleviated by concurrent closures of downstream converters' plants and holiday-related shutdowns. Nevertheless, the repercussions on supply and demand are expected to become more evident as purchasing interest revives in the upcoming weeks. It is noteworthy that restoring operations at converters' plants is projected to be relatively easier than re-establishing full capacity at crackers and polymer units. Meanwhile, maintenance work has commenced at a cracker in France, while another cracker in northern France is still offline due to a separate power outage that caused technical difficulties. Reports indicate that this unit may remain out of operation for several weeks, potentially impacting associated polyethylene and polypropylene units. Furthermore, maintenance is currently underway at two HDPE plants in Central and Eastern Europe. PP: This week, PP contract prices plummeted, while spot prices remained constant in the European region. Contract prices in the European polypropylene (PP) market decreased, reflecting weak demand and expectations of additional deterioration in May. The negative sentiment was fueled by slow consumption in major sectors and a widely expected drop in the May monthly contract price for propylene, the main feedstock for polypropylene. Market activity continued to be quiet, with limited buying interest and cautious purchasing behavior from downstream customers. A market participant remarked that any declines in monomer costs would be completely passed along the value chain to downstream purchasers, indicating further weakening of PP prices in the short term. Participants pointed out that the overall sentiment was hindered by ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties, elevated inflation in certain parts of Europe, and a sluggish recovery in end-use sectors like automotive and construction. In light of these circumstances, sellers are increasingly struggling to sustain margins, particularly as significant import pressures from Asia and the Middle East remain. Weak demand fundamentals persisted in hampering spot market activity in the European polypropylene (PP) sector. Although supply levels remained unchanged, market participants had little optimism for a significant demand recovery in the next month. Buyers stayed cautious, opting for a hand-to-mouth strategy amid persistent macroeconomic uncertainties and subdued downstream offtake. Import activity, especially from the Middle East, attracted attention as competitive pricing began to impact domestic offers. Saudi-origin homopolymer PP was reportedly accessible at reduced prices when compared to European material, leading to increased interest from converters and traders. The relative strength of the euro against the US dollar also contributed to enhancing import attractiveness. Due to a weaker dollar, the effective cost of Middle Eastern imports has significantly decreased, nearly eliminating the effect of import duties and creating more advantageous conditions for foreign sellers. This currency situation has enabled higher polypropylene inflows into the European market, leading to increased competitive pressure on local producers. PS: This week, PS prices trended sharply lower s in the European region. In the past week, European polystyrene spot and contract prices experienced a decline following announcements from producers regarding their price reduction objectives. This downward pressure on prices was exacerbated by the styrene monomer May contract price, which fell by Euro 40/mt compared to the previous month. Market trading activity remained subdued as participants began negotiations for contract prices for the month. Early May demand has been limited due to public holidays across Europe; however, overall demand for the month is anticipated to remain stable, akin to April's levels. The downstream sectors continue to face challenges from macroeconomic factors, including inflation and interest rates, yet demand for 2025 is projected to remain relatively flat without significant fluctuations. Polystyrene supply is expected to remain balanced to long in May, with suppliers likely to calibrate production activity with demand. In April, some import offers from Asia were noted for May delivery, and while there were expectations of increased import competition, clarity on this matter is still lacking at this early stage, compounded by long lead times. There is ongoing uncertainty regarding the potential effects of US tariffs on the supply chain and upstream styrene, leading many market participants to adopt a cautious approach. Demand from the food packaging sector has remained stable week over week, while the insulation market continues to face subdued conditions. Converters have reported a decrease in order volumes due to limited activity in the construction industry. A weakening US dollar has enhanced the competitiveness of imports relative to domestic materials. Some market participants believe that a downward trend in pricing and prompt delivery could enhance the appeal of European materials compared to imports. ABS: This week, ABS spot prices slipped, while contract prices remaining flat in the European region. The prices of ABS in Europe have shown a downward trend, influenced by a bearish market environment. High production rates and an increase in imports from Asia have led to competitive pricing in the market. Concurrently, players in the European ABS market have raised concerns regarding a potential surge of products from China, particularly in light of the ongoing investigation into South Korean and UNV1 ABS exports to the EU. The tariffs imposed on China by the previous administration may lead to an influx of Chinese ABS into the European market. Nevertheless, the European market is likely to remain stable, as exporting ABS from China to the US has become prohibitively expensive, rendering Chinese products uncompetitive in Europe. Demand for ABS downstream has remained subdued, particularly as the construction sector has not yet resumed its seasonal demand. Additionally, declining feedstock prices have further pressured ABS prices. The European ABS market has also been affected by the permanent closure of the Propylene Oxide/styrene Monomer (POSM) unit at the Maasvlakte plant in the Netherlands, a decision driven by chronic overcapacity, increased imports from Asia, and high production levels within Europe. Consequently, participants in the European ABS market are encountering considerable difficulties in maintaining profitability. In spite of the correction on the supply side, the demand from the downstream automotive and appliance sectors in the region has remained lacklustre. The European manufacturing sector has seen a slow recovery with only slight improvements. While some growth has been noted, new orders remain feeble, and industrial activity has yet to fully recover. The UK's industrial production has continued to decline, further deepening its negative trajectory, which adversely affects ABS demand. The ABS market is likely to remain under strain as supply continues to exceed demand. In Europe, ongoing structural challenges, including competition from Asian imports, will likely keep prices stable. Although certain supply adjustments are underway, the fundamental demand remains weak, and a price rebound in the near future is not anticipated. PVC: This week, PVC contract prices declined, while spot prices remained constant in the European region. The European polyvinyl chloride contract prices trended lower this week due to a decrease in the European ethylene May contract price settlement, coupled with the persistently low demand for PVC further exacerbating the market outlook.. Both producers and consumers anticipate that market conditions in May will mirror those of April, characterized by gradual yet stable demand and slight supply constraints. Buyers have observed a lack of increase in downstream demand, attributed to subdued construction activity and consumer spending. Following the price settlement, negotiations commenced in the PVC contract market between producers and consumers. Producers emphasized the necessity of improving margins, describing current margins as unsustainable. Conversely, consumers advocated for more significant price reductions in light of the declining ethylene market contract price for May and the weak PVC demand from downstream sectors. Looking ahead, industry sources predict a rise in demand in May compared to April, although the Easter holidays are believed to have dampened market activity. Supply in Europe remains somewhat limited, with outages reported in both France and Poland. A facility in Pernis, Netherlands, is expected to restart operations, with PVC deliveries anticipated for May. A site in France is projected to resume production by the end of May, while a facility in Poland is currently undergoing maintenance following a technical issue during its restart. This week, offers in the spot market have decreased, particularly for Chinese materials, which are now more affordable as compared to other Asian materials from Korea and Taiwan. As regards imports, a varied situation has emerged; some sources reported diminished interest due to the decline in the European ethylene MCP for May. Conversely, other market participants noted that the recent appreciation of the euro against the US dollar, coupled with weak domestic demand in Asia, has created more appealing opportunities. Increased interest in imported grades has been observed this week from buyers who were previously hesitant about importing volumes. In Spain, market conditions are stabilizing following the power outage; however, sources indicate that some supply disruptions persist. In Turkey, weak demand continues amid a challenging political and economic environment. European producers have maintained lower pricing since the beginning of the year to remain competitive against affordably priced materials from the US and Asia. The downward trend in crude oil prices is contributing to lower energy costs and affecting the overall petrochemicals market, which is expected to lower production costs in May. This situation may also encourage a more cautious approach among petrochemical buyers, who anticipate that price reductions will eventually be reflected in downstream markets. Meanwhile, demand for Emulsion PVC (e-PVC) in northwest Europe remains weak, particularly as the automotive industry, a key end-use market for e-PVC, has faced increasing pressure in recent months. PET: This week, PET spot prices drifted lower sharply, while contract prices remained flat in the European region. The European PET markets commenced May with significant price reductions, continuing the downward trend from April. Spot prices fell during the week due to persistently weak demand and anticipated inflows of imported materials. The expectation of incoming import volumes led buyers to postpone purchases in anticipation of more favorable pricing. Prices also decreased, largely as a result of sluggish demand from downstream sectors and cautious procurement practices within the bottle and food packaging industries. Despite a stable supply and unchanged feedstock prices (PTA and MEG), weak consumer confidence and limited end-user activity hindered any upward price movements. Additionally, logistical challenges and moderate export activities further dampened buying interest, contributing to a softening price environment. However, a regional producer warned that container shipments were experiencing delays of up to a month in some instances, with significant congestion at the port of Rotterdam. The source indicated that if import volumes face delays, market conditions could shift rapidly. Local offers emerged with reductions while import offers also declined, reflecting heightened competition from Asian suppliers. This month has seen increased pressure, with notable declines driving spot PET bottle prices down to levels not seen in over four years as May begins. PET consumers in Europe maintained adequate stock levels as the typically high-demand preform season started more slowly than anticipated. The pricing of PET in Europe was affected by both cost pressures and weak demand. A decrease in feedstock costs is expected to lead to a market reversal in the coming months. External factors, including fluctuating crude oil prices and cautious downstream demand, continued to keep overall market sentiment subdued. The prevailing macroeconomic uncertainty, exacerbated by seasonal influences, led most consumers to prioritize necessary purchases over speculative ones. Consequently, PET prices faced ongoing pressure with few chances for substantial recovery. In summary, the interplay of macroeconomic factors and variations in feedstock prices contributed to a subdued PET market. USHDPE: This week, HDPE export prices drifted lower in the US. During the week, spot export prices for high density polyethylene (HDPE) decreased for both blowmolding and film grades, while prices for injection grades remained stable. Although purchasing activity has begun to increase, numerous customers are advocating for reduced prices due to consistently elevated inventory levels. The blowmolding-grade HDPE experienced the most significant pricing pressure, influenced by competitive offers from traders and intensified competition in the export market. As the market transitioned into May, the sentiment continued to be overshadowed by uncertainty, with participants noting persistent logistical challenges that are affecting trade flows and overall confidence. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices down adjusted in the US. The prices of low density polyethylene (LDPE) experienced a decrease over the past week, indicating persistent weakness in demand. Nevertheless, there has been a slight uptick in purchasing interest relative to early April. As the market approaches May, the outlook for export activities remains ambiguous, with continuous pressure for reduced prices affecting overall confidence. Reports regarding inventory levels were varied�some sources indicated that warehouses are close to full capacity, while others observed a slight reduction in stockpiles compared to the more congested conditions of April. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices slipped in the US. During the past week, US spot export prices for linear low density polyethylene (LLDPE) have decreased, as market participants have noted lower offers in the export sector compared to previous weeks, driven by persistent weakness in global demand. Present pricing from producers has reached historic lows, and many have stated that additional price cuts are unlikely. Although some market participants express cautious optimism regarding a possible recovery in May, others are more sceptical, pointing out that ongoing market instability may allow for further declines if circumstances do not get better. PP: This week, PP export prices raced higher in the US. US spot export polypropylene (PP) prices experienced an increase in the week concluded, driven by a revival in demand from Mexico, which continues to be the most advantageous netback market for US-sourced PP. Following a lacklustre first half of 2025�characterized by uncertainties regarding tariffs and strong competition from China and the Middle East�market dynamics seem to be evolving. Buyers in Mexico, who had been hesitant for several months, have begun to re-enter the market, resulting in a resurgence of purchasing activity. Industry insiders remarked that everyone had been waiting for an extended period to make purchases, indicating a release of accumulated demand. Furthermore, specific end-use sectors, particularly nonwovens, have demonstrated signs of recovery. However, China remains the leading player in the global PP market, benefiting from its extensive production capabilities and aggressive pricing tactics. PS: This week, PS export prices rolled over in the US. Polystyrene (PS) prices in the US, based on FAS Houston basis, remained stable throughout the week, while the import prices for expandable polystyrene (EPS) experienced a decrease. Market participants observed a change in demand patterns, with some buyers reportedly opting for alternative materials such as polypropylene and PET instead of PS, a trend that may further reduce PS consumption. In light of these changes, major manufacturers like INEOS Styrolution decided to maintain PS prices for May. Nevertheless, segments of the market are anticipating a possible price decrease later in the month, pointing to the recent drop in benzene prices as a potential catalyst for a decline in PS pricing. PVC: This week, PVC export prices inched higher in the US. The US polyvinyl chloride (PVC) market has been facing ongoing supply constraints this week, leading to a slight increase in spot prices after reaching historic lows at the end of April. However, this minor price rise has not alleviated the overall market sentiment, which remains burdened by consistently low demand and high inventory levels at downstream buyers' facilities. These negative factors, combined with broader macroeconomic challenges and specific market pressures, have kept prices under downward pressure for several months, bringing spot values to levels reminiscent of the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Nonetheless, some market participants are expressing cautious optimism, suggesting that the market may have reached its lowest point and are hopeful for a gradual recovery in prices as market fundamentals start to stabilize. PET: This week, PET prices continued to remain stable in the US. During the week ending May 7, the spot import prices for polyethylene terephthalate (PET) in the United States remained unchanged, reflecting limited market activity due to ongoing trade uncertainties. Participants in the market exhibited caution, with many choosing to delay significant purchases while they seek clearer indications of future pricing trends. The outlook appears uncertain, as several stakeholders are opting for a wait-and-see strategy. In the upstream market, attention continues to be focused on the pending settlements for March contract prices of feedstock Paraxylene (PX) and Purified terephthalic acid (PTA), which are typically finalized in the last week of the contract month or the first week of the subsequent month.

data.title: Russia says Western arms flows to Ukraine would need to stop during any ceasefire
data.description:
Russia would require a halt to U.S. and European arms supplies to Ukraine during any potential ceasefire, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said in an interview with ABC News.

"Otherwise it will be an advantage for Ukraine. Ukraine will continue their total mobilisation, bringing new troops to (the) frontline," Peskov said.

"Ukraine will use this period to train new military personnel and to give a rest to their existing ones. So why should we grant such an advantage to Ukraine?"

U.S. President Donald Trump urged Russia and Ukraine on Friday to "get this stupid war finished", as he pushed for a 30-day ceasefire that Ukraine says it is willing to agree to.

But Peskov, in the interview, restated Russian concerns that Putin had made public as far back as March 13 and set out in a phone call with Trump on March 18.

"A ceasefire was supported by President Putin, but he asked several questions. He said that right now we have certain dynamics on the front, Russian troops are advancing, and advancing in quite a confident way," he said.

"So if we speak about ceasefire, what are we going to do with shipments of weapons coming every day from the United States and from European countries?"

His comments underlined the lack of any shift in Russia's position on a ceasefire since mid-March. During that time Trump, who previously had applied intense pressure on Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, has shown growing impatience with Russia and questioned whether Putin was "tapping me along".

Peskov denied that was the case, and said it was Ukraine that was refusing to enter direct negotiations.

"President Putin is doing whatever is possible to solve the problem, to achieve a settlement through peaceful and diplomatic means. But having no peaceful and diplomatic means at hand, we have to continue the military operation," he said.

Russia hoped that mediation by Trump would help to bring "a little bit more flexibility, a little bit more political will and wisdom to the Kyiv regime", Peskov said.

Putin declared a brief Easter ceasefire last month, which both sides accused each other of violating countless times, and another three-day pause this week as Russia marks the 80th anniversary of victory in World War Two. Ukraine did not agree to the latest truce, saying it wanted a halt of 30 days or more.

data.title: Stellar (XLM) Breakout Eyes 30% Rally – Will It Repeat Last Cycle’s Playbook?
data.description:
After breaking out of its five-month downtrend, an analyst suggests a repeat of Stellar (XLM)’s last cycle playbook could be on the horizon. The cryptocurrency has confirmed its breakdown from a bullish reversal pattern and eyes a surge toward new targets

Stellar Breakout Targets $0.39

Amid the market pump, Stellar has broken out of a key demand zone and retested the $0.30 mark for the first time since March. The cryptocurrency has been in a downtrend since its November 2024 breakout, when it reached a three-year high of $0.63.

During this year’s retraces, XLM dropped 68% from the highs to a five-month low of $0.20. However, the late-April market recovery saw the cryptocurrency surge above the downtrend and attempt to confirm the breakout after recording a weekly close above the $0.28 mark.

On Friday, Stellar has reclaimed the $0.29 resistance and retested the $0.30 mark for the first time in nearly two months. Following today’s performance, Ali Martinez pointed out that Stellar is breaking out of a two-month inverse head and Shoulder pattern.

This pattern is a bullish reversal setup that suggests a potential shift from a downtrend to an uptrend. Earlier this week, the analyst pointed out that the pattern’s right shoulder was forming and the neckline sat around the $0.29 mark.

According to his post, a breakout from this formation potentially eyed a 30% rally toward the $0.39 resistance, lost during the February retraces.

XLM To Repeat Historical Tendencies?

Analyst Rekt Capital highlighted that the cryptocurrency confirmed the end of its multi-month downtrend and a breakout from its Downtrending Channel.

Per the post, if XLM weekly closes above its key area, between $0.27-$0.29, any dips into this zone would figure as a successful reclaim of the area as support to support a move to higher regions.

The analyst explained that reclaiming the $0.27-$0.29 area is crucial because it is a “historical demand region on the monthly timeframe.” In the past, turning this zone into support during bull markets has enabled Stellar to rally toward the $0.37-$0.40 mark.

In 2021, the cryptocurrency rallied toward its cycle high of $0.80 after retesting the key demand zone and breaking out of the $0.37 barrier. Similarly, it hit its all-time high (ATH) of $0.87 after a breakout from this area.

If XLM repeats history and rallies to the next resistance, it must reclaim and confirm that level to continue with its historical tendencies. “As such, a reclaim could see XLM challenge the $0.52 blue highs over time,” Rekt Capital concluded.

Meanwhile, analyst CW has noted that after breaking the upper line of the downtrend channel, Stellar would encounter resistance in two selling walls, one between the $0.34-$0.38 levels, and a big one around the $0.47-$0.70 zone.

As of this writing, Stellar trades at $0.296, a 2% increase in the daily timeframe.

data.title: China Talks Are Better Than Nothing, But Don't Expect Much of a Thaw — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Brian P. Klein

About the author: Brian P. Klein is the founder of RidgePoint Global, a strategic advisory firm, and a former U.S. diplomat.

Expectations for a thaw in the U.S.-China trade war are rising faster than reason supports. Unless tariff rates fall considerably, bilateral trade will continue to grind to a near halt. Repairing the economic damage is the only outcome that really matters.

Nearly everyone, from Wall Street traders to business leaders to anxious U.S. consumers, are banking on a breakthrough in Geneva, where U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, and China Vice Premier He Lifeng are meeting this week to begin trade talks. Even the mercurial Bitcoin is pushing toward new highs on this news. These talks, however, will most likely just establish a framework for later negotiations. The Trump administration will tout that as a success. But that isn't going to stem concerns about a U.S. recession, which some business leaders think is already underway.

Even if tariffs on some goods are symbolically lowered to give the impression that tensions are ratcheting down more than just a notch, they'd have to fall below roughly 35% for Chinese producers to become profitable trading with the U.S. again. President Donald Trump floated the idea of reducing tariffs to 80% on Friday, well above that threshold.

The president has sent other conflicting messages about trade negotiations recently. He said during a meeting with Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney that countries have to sign a deal with Trump because they want a piece of the U.S. market. Then, in an ABC interview, he said that China deserved the near-embargo and would likely "eat the tariffs." Days later he signaled that he would meet with Xi if the Geneva talks are successful. Equivocation isn't a strong negotiating tactic, especially if the other side sees it as weakness.

So far there is no indication that either side is willing to make significant concessions, let alone take the necessary steps toward reconciliation. Bessent has tried to tamp down overly optimistic expectations. These coming talks "will be about de-escalation, not about the big trade deal," he said this week.

Meeting may be better than nothing, but the margin for success is awfully slim. The consequences of tariffs maintained at anywhere near current levels are a significant headwind for the U.S., China, and the broader global trading system.

U.S. businesses are anxious for a substantive deal. They abhor uncertainty, especially when they need to make major purchasing and investment decisions that are difficult to unwind. Major retailers like Walmart and Target have privately warned of empty shelves if tariffs don't drop soon. Others are pulling quarterly guidance or warning of tougher times to come, despite U.S. equity markets rising with irrational exuberance in recent weeks.

Many CEOs may be happy with an agreement to talk more in the future — that would at least take some of the market pressure off their shoulders. But the "hope put," as it were, isn't going to be enough to counter inflation, supply shortages, or the political fallout of very expensive Christmas gifts. And for Trump, telling hardworking Americans that they need to take one for the billionaires and eat a little hardship while prices go up, purchasing power goes down, and store shelves grow empty will have political consequences.

China is also feeling the strain as factory orders drop substantially and the prospects of layoffs rise. Its drop-off in exports are staggering. Forty-one percent of Hong Kong container shipments to the U.S. West Coast have been canceled, starting May 12. Developing export markets for Chinese firms in the Middle East and Africa may absorb a bit of the manufacturing largess from lost U.S. sales, but they can't possibly replace the enormous buying power and diversity of goods required by the U.S. market.

Considering the costs of a protracted trade war, one would surmise that both sides, at least in private, would be eager for a face-saving deal. Trump and Xi need to be able to claim to be the toughest negotiator, the country that has lasted longest in this reckless game of trade chicken, and to have the strongest economy that can withstand higher prices. But public posturing won't be enough to fix the unfolding economic damage.

Behind closed doors, Trump's negotiators may be more frank in their discussions. And if they don't treat China as an equal, the talks will be very short. Even the slightest sense of being "lectured to" or coerced will quickly evaporate any goodwill generated from simply agreeing to meet with the Chinese.

While the U.S. can't realistically negotiate a fully baked free-trade agreement or a "get to zero" agreement in the short term, the Trump administration does need something resembling actual progress to substantially increase business certainty and restore access to critical goods.

Based on the U.K. deal announced this week, both seem out of reach. The relatively simple framework agreement lacked both clarity and substance. It declared intentions to work on a number of issues, without actually committing to them.

Investors and businesses will see through vague wording and consumers will remain laser-focused on prices. Anything short of a significant deal with China will provide little solace for a dangerously ailing economy.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com .

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
